elements.sent-2411-001: Let $R$ and $S$ be commutative rings with identity, $f:R\to S$ a ring homomorphism and $J$ an ideal of $S$.
elements.sent-2411-002: Then the subring $R\bowtie^fJ:=\{(r,f(r)+j)\mid r\in R$ and $j\in J\}$ of $R\times S$ is called the amalgamation of $R$ with $S$ along $J$ with respect to $f$.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we generalize and improve recent results on the computation of the diameter of the zero-divisor graph of amalgamated algebras and obtain new results.
elements.sent-2411-004: In particular, we provide new characterizations for completeness of the zero-divisor graph of amalgamated algebra, as well as, a complete description for the diameter of the zero-divisor graph of amalgamations in the special case of finite rings.
elements.sent-2411-005: The concept of the graph of the zero-divisors of a ring $R$ was introduced by Beck in \cite{b and further studied by Anderson and Naseer in \cite{an .}}
elements.sent-2411-006: In their work, all elements of the ring were vertices of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-007: Then, in \cite{al , Anderson and Livingston considered only the set of non-zero zero-divisors of $R$ as vertices.}
elements.sent-2411-008: The definition of Anderson and Livingston (which we adopt in this paper) is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-009: The zero-divisor graph of $R$, denoted by $\Gamma (R)$, is the undirected graph with vertices $\Z(R)^\bullet=\Z(R)\setminus \{0\}$, and for distinct $x,y \in \Z(R)^\bullet$, the vertices $x$ and $y$ are adjacent if and only if $xy = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-010: Recall that the distance between (connected) vertices $x$ and $y$, denoted by $d(x,y)$, is the length of a shortest path connecting them.
elements.sent-2411-011: The diameter of a connected graph $G$, denoted by $\diam (G)$, is the supremum of the distances between vertices.
elements.sent-2411-013: D'Anna, Finocchiaro, and Fontana in \cite{DFF and \cite{DFF2 have introduced the following construction.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Let $R$ and $S$ be two commutative rings with identity, let $J$ be an ideal of $S$ and let $f:R\to S$ be a ring homomorphism.
elements.sent-2411-015: They introduced the following subring (with standard component-wise operations) \begin{equation}R\bowtie^fJ:=\{(r,f(r)+j)\mid r\in R and j\in J\}\end{equation} of $R\times S$, called the amalgamated algebra (or amalgamation) of $R$ with $S$ along $J$ with respect to $f$.
elements.sent-2411-016: This construction generalizes the amalgamated duplication of a ring along an ideal (introduced and studied in \cite{DF ).}

elements.sent-2411-020: In 1955, Nagata introduced the idealization of $M$ in $R$ (or the trivial extension of $R$ by $M$), denoted here by $R\ltimes M$.
elements.sent-2411-021: In \cite{as06 , Axtell and Stickles studied the diameter and girth of the zero-divisor graph of a ring when extending to idealizations of the ring.}

elements.sent-2411-023: In \cite{y , Maimani and Yassemi investigated the diameter and girth of the zero-divisor graph of the ring $R\bowtie I$.}

elements.sent-2411-026: In this paper we pursue previous works on the diameter of the zero-divisor graph of amalgamations.
elements.sent-2411-027: Our attempt improves and recovers some of previous results and yields new results on amalgamated algebra, $n$-trivial extension (see definition in the following) and idealization.
elements.sent-2411-028: The outline of the paper is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-029: In Section 2 we fix our notation and give some elementary results on which we base our approach.
elements.sent-2411-030: In Section 3, under certain assumptions, we give equivalent conditions for completeness of $\Gamma (\rr)$.
elements.sent-2411-031: Section 4 provides conditions under which the diameter of the zero-divisor graph of amalgamations is $2$ or $3$.
elements.sent-2411-032: In Section 5, we give a complete description of the diameter of the zero-divisor graph of amalgamations in the special case of finite rings.
elements.sent-2411-033: Our results generalize and complete well-known results mentioned above, and yield new and original examples of graphs of diameters $1$, $2$, and $3$.
elements.sent-2411-034: To facilitate the reading of the paper, in this section, we recall definitions and prove some preliminary properties we need later.

elements.sent-2411-043: Let $M=(M_i)_{i=1 ^n$ be a family of $R$-modules and $\varphi=\{\varphi_{i,j}\}$ be a family of bilinear maps such that each $\varphi_{i,j}$ is written multiplicatively: \begin{equation}\varphi_{i,j}: M_i\times M_j \longrightarrow M_{i+j} (m_i,m_j) \longmapsto \varphi_{i,j}(m_i,m_j):=m_im_j\end{equation} The $n$-trivial extension of $R$ by $M$ is the set denoted by $R\ltimes_nM$ whose underlying additive group is $R\oplus M_1\oplus\cdots\oplus M_n$ with multiplication given by \begin{equation}(m_0,\ldots,m_n)(m'_0,\ldots,m'_n)=\Big(\sum_{j+k=i} m_jm'_k\Big)_i\end{equation} for all $(m_0,\ldots,m_n),(m'_0,\ldots,m'_n)\in R\ltimes_nM$.}
elements.sent-2411-044: It is shown that $R\ltimes_nM$ is associative ring precisely when $(m_im_j)m_k=m_i(m_jm_k)$ for $m_i\in M_i, m_j\in M_j$ and $m_k\in M_k$ with $1\leq i, j, k \leq n - 2$ and $i + j + k\leq n$.
elements.sent-2411-045: Further, for $R\ltimes_nM$ to be a commutative ring with identity we need $R$ to be commutative with identity and $m_im_j=m_jm_i$ for every $1\leq i, j \leq n-1$ with $i+j\leq n$.
